title: Are Quick Delivery Apps Making It Easier to Buy Medicines Without Prescription?
description: Deceiving to sell: As medicine delivery through e-comm and quick-commerce apps becomes faster, experts say the systems designed to protect patients must keep pace too.
content: Ratish Sharma was out of his blood pressure medication, Telma 40, when he realised he could order it through a popular quick-commerce app. After selecting the medicine, a message appeared that an in-house doctor would call to verify the order and he should keep the prescription handy. He didn't have the prescription so when the call came, the 'doctor' asked a few basic questions like his age and how long he had been taking the medicine. Within seconds, the order was approved. Less than 10 minutes later, a strip of Telma 40 arrived at his doorstep in good condition. On paper, the platform followed its process but the experience raises a larger question: Are these checks sufficient for medicines that legally require a prescription? If someone without hypertension were to answer those questions convincingly, could they obtain a prescription-only medicine just as easily? Could a brief verification call replace a doctor's assessment of a patient's medical history, kidney function, current medications and whether the drug is appropriate in the first place? Moreover, until the medicines arrive, there is no way to verify its condition or packaging.                   These questions are becoming increasingly relevant as quick-commerce platforms expand beyond groceries to healthcare. The convenience they offer is undeniable, especially for elderly patients, people with chronic illnesses and those who may find it difficult to visit a pharmacy. But leading doctors say that while medicines should become easier to access, one cannot bypass prescription safeguards.To understand where the balance should lie, Times Now Digital spoke to physicians and internal medicine specialists about the risks of buying prescription medicines online, what consumers should know about expiry dates and the simple precautions everyone should take before ordering medicines through e-pharmacies and quick-commerce platforms."Prescription regulations exist primarily to protect patients by ensuring medicines are used safely and appropriately," says Dr Amit Prakash Singh, Consultant - Internal Medicine, CK Birla Hospital, Delhi. He says that medicines such as Telma 40 (telmisartan) are prescribed only after considering several factors, including a person's blood pressure, kidney function, medical history and other medications they may be taking. "When prescription medicines are supplied without proper medical evaluation, there is a risk that the person may be taking the wrong drug, the wrong dose or using it for an inappropriate condition. This can lead to complications such as excessively low blood pressure, dizziness, fainting, kidney dysfunction or dangerous interactions with other medicines. Self-medication may also delay the diagnosis of an underlying health problem that requires proper medical attention," he says.Dr Khazi Javeed Irfan, Senior Consultant, Internal Medicine, SPARSH Hospital, Bengaluru, agrees that a prescription is much more than a formality. Medicines prescribed for chronic conditions often require regular monitoring, something a quick verification call cannot assess. "A person may unknowingly consume the wrong dose, combine it with unsuitable medicines or use it despite kidney disease, low blood pressure or electrolyte imbalance. Prescription medicines need medical supervision because their safety depends on the patient's overall condition, medical history and follow-up," he says.According to Dr Ishwar Gilada, Mumbai-based physician and infectious diseases expert, the risks extend well beyond blood pressure medicines. Easy access to prescription drugs can encourage self-medication, potentially masking serious illnesses, delaying diagnosis and exposing patients to harmful drug interactions or allergic reactions. He also cautions that unrestricted access to antibiotics contributes to antimicrobial resistance, while prolonged unsupervised use of medicines such as sleeping pills, anti-anxiety drugs and opioid painkillers can lead to dependence.                            The doctors also urge consumers not to overlook expiry dates, especially when medicines are ordered online and this is a big limitation of ordering medicines online is that consumers often have little control over which specific strip they receive. Unlike at a physical pharmacy, where a buyer can check the packaging and expiry before purchasing, online orders are typically packed and dispatched without giving customers an opportunity to verify such details. By the time the medicine reaches the doorstep, the transaction has already been completed, making it all the more important for consumers to inspect the packaging before consuming the medicine.While accidentally taking an expired medicine is unlikely to cause immediate harm in most cases, its effectiveness may be reduced. "Expiry dates indicate the period during which manufacturers can guarantee a medicine's safety, quality and potency when stored correctly," says Dr Amit Prakash Singh.Not all medicines behave the same after expiry. Many tablets and capsules gradually lose potency, but products such as insulin, vaccines, biologics, eye drops, nitroglycerin preparations and reconstituted liquid antibiotics can deteriorate much faster and should never be used beyond their expiry date.Dr Sufi Roomi, Medical Spokesperson, Jolly Healthcare, says consumers should always inspect medicines after delivery. "Verify the expiry date, batch number, packaging seal and bill. Medicines with a short remaining shelf life may not be suitable for long-term treatment, while damaged packaging may indicate improper storage," he says.Dr Ishwar Gilada adds that people should avoid unnecessary panic if they realise they have accidentally taken an expired medicine. "By taking an expired medicine, you do not expire," he says. The actual risk depends on the medicine involved, how long it has been expired and how it was stored. However, medicines relied upon in emergencies such as insulin or nitroglycerin, should never be used after their expiry date because even a loss of potency can have life-threatening consequences.Ultimately, doctors agree that while quick-commerce and e-pharmacy platforms have transformed access to medicines, especially for patients managing chronic illnesses, convenience should not replace clinical judgement. As medicine delivery becomes faster, experts say the systems designed to protect patients must keep pace too.

title: Motoring groups split on whether Fuel Finder cuts prices at the pump
description:
UK government launched service in February to help drivers compare the price of fuel at nearby filling stations

Motoring and industry groups say it remains unclear whether Fuel Finder, the UK government scheme that lets drivers compare fuel prices at nearby petrol stations, is influencing where motorists choose to fill up.
The service, which is available through apps and websites including Waze, Petrol Prices and the AA, was launched four months ago. It now covers around 95% of UK forecourts, according to the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ).
The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) claims motorists could save up to £9 a tank by switching from the most expensive to the cheapest retailer within a 10-minute drive. 
Geoff Steadman, a senior director at the CMA, said: "We know higher prices at the pump in recent months are putting real pressure on drivers’ pockets, so using Fuel Finder-backed services will enable them to find the cheapest petrol station nearby and save money.
“But we’re concerned that weak competition in the sector is leaving drivers paying more. By making prices more transparent, Fuel Finder should push petrol stations to compete harder and drive down prices in the long run.”
However, the RAC and the Petrol Retailers Association say it's too early to judge whether or not the scheme is working.
Simon Williams, the RAC’s head of policy, told Autocar that “the jury is still out” and “the price of diesel should really have fallen faster” – but said he was “hopeful that it could make a difference”. 
Gordon Balmer, the executive director of the Petrol Retailers Association, said he doesn’t believe that Fuel Finder has “had a material effect on buyer behaviour”.
“Prior to its launch, consumers already knew that supermarkets price their fuel keenly and that you will pay a little more on the motorways,” he said. 
“At the start of the crisis in the Middle East, supermarkets experienced a spike in fuel sales as consumers filled up to try and beat price increases, however I don't think that was due to improved price visibility.”
Balmer said that it was up to the government “to assess its success” but confirmed that he hadn't seen any data including usage of fuel pricing apps.
Is Fuel Finder actually helping?
The AA takes a different view, however. Luke Bosdet, its head of policy, observed that many motorway service areas cut their prices when petrol wholesale prices were rising. 
He said: “Petrol at 185 pence a litre earlier on in the [Iran] war is now 176 pence at notoriously expensive locations like Chieveley [Berkshire] on the M4.”
He attributed this to Fuel Finder: “We have been surprised at the speed of the reductions and concluded that this is the influence of Fuel Finder: retailers seeing how much rivals are cutting their prices and, knowing that drivers can see the same information, having to respond.”
DESNZ didn't answer questions about how many motorists use Fuel Finder-backed services or whether the scheme is influencing where drivers buy fuel.
A spokesperson said Fuel Finder was "playing a crucial part in our work to tackle the cost of living head on", though, adding that the department estimated it could save households that own a car around £40 a year.
The CMA said it expected to publish a more detailed assessment of Fuel Finder's impact later this year as it continues to examine competition in the fuel market.
Autocar also contacted VE3, which built the platform, for this information, but it hadn’t responded at the time of writing.

title: Why JLR expects growth in China while rivals are suffering
description:
Long-wheelbase Jaguar XF is still among JLR's best-sellers in China


JLR got ahead of pain points hitting rivals in China and now is ready to roll out value-over-volume plan

Most European premium brands, including BMW and Porsche, have suffered badly in China in recent months, but not all.
JLR was actually bullish about its future in the country at its recent investor day held in June. So how did a company that has experienced well-documented problems in the world’s largest car market in recent years finally get it right?
“Unlike other OEMs, we are not signalling a problem in China. Now I expect to start seeing growth coming back into that market for us,” CEO PB Balaji told investors.
The market has long proved a rich seam of gold for JLR’s imported models, leading with the Range Rover, Range Rover Sport and Land Rover Defender. That was still the case in the company’s financial year ending March, when the average selling price of its imports – mainly from the UK – hit £105,609. 
That figure was up by over £10,000 from five years ago in a market that is famously driven by discounts.
“Today we sell 16,000 Range Rovers there annually without batting an eyelid, and we are amongst the lowest in terms of VMEs [discounts] that we put in that market,” Balaji said.
Look at sales figures alone and JLR’s situation looks as bad as that of BMW, which issued a profit warning in June after sales took an unexpectedly big hit in China, down 15% to the end of May.
JLR overall retail sales in the country fell 25% to 62,400 in the financial year, a loss of some 20,000, with a greater fall of 35% in the three months to the end of March. 
But in that figure are almost 25,000 vehicles built locally by its joint venture with Chery, led by the Range Rover Evoque and – still – the long-wheelbase Jaguar XF.
That side of the business has long suffered amid the “killing fields” of mass premium, as CFO Richard Molyneux memorably described it, with discounts dragging down the average selling price of the smaller Chinese-built models to just £34,738 in the last financial year.
“The challenge in the market happened in terms of the lower-end cars,” Balaji said.
That joint-venture side of the business helped pushed China to become JLR’s biggest global market, selling a record 87,774 cars built in the Changshu, Shanghai, plant in the 2018 financial year.
But it has often come at immense cost to the company, most famously contributing to a £3.1 billion writedown in the 2019 financial year after overstuffing the Chinese market.
Only in recent years have the joint-venture losses been stemmed after JLR cut back on volumes, but profits have been small and inconsistent, most recently £10 million in the 2026 financial year. It hasn’t generated a dividend for JLR in the last five years.
The smart solution that JLR has devised is to hand the development of models for the plant over to Chery, which earlier this year launched the first of a “portfolio” of Freelander models to built there. JLR receives a royalty payment and gets to gracefully exit a 12-year chapter in its history that generated mainly headaches and not much profit. Production of the last JLR models in China – including the Jaguar XE and XF – will end in September.
Essentially JLR has already been through the pain and solved the problem that is just starting to properly hit the bigger premiums like BMW and Audi. Their bigger footprint in China and the country's continued outsized influence on the balance sheet foretells a larger battle ahead, as premium customers continue to shift to electrified models from Chinese brands and away from the mainly ICE offerings from European brands.
JLR can now follow Porsche in restricting sales to high-end valuable imports via its shrunken dealer network at a level the market will take, without needing to push too hard.
“With the volumes of the [locally-built] models also going off, it's a much smaller but more profitable and extremely powerful set of products there in the market,” Balaji said. “It's more about 'now build back, but build back gradually. Don't be in a hurry, because the market will take its time to settle itself.'”
JLR is now looking to the US – its new biggest market – to provide the bulk of luxury sales that will drive margins in coming years.
One analyst pointed out that back in 2022 JLR had made the same claims for China, which didn’t materialise.
“The market has also changed dramatically,” Balaji said. “I don't think in '22 there was a major callout in terms of the local OEMs going up to a 60%-plus market share.”
Problems remain. JLR’s import business in China is now all ICE-powered after it pulled plug-in hybrids from the market, along with all other European premium brands.
For now luxury customers are content to stick to ICE but even that’s dropping, with JLR wholesales (ie its import business) down 27% last year and 30% in the three months to the end of March.
The delayed Range Rover and Range Rover Sport electric models are coming, potentially by the end of the year, but it remains to be seen how persuasive they will be against some pretty impressive local competition.
Meanwhile changes to the 10% luxury tax China imposed last July dragged in all Range Rovers after the threshold shifted from ¥1.3m (£145,000) to ¥900,000 (just over £100,000). JLR admitted that, like the US tariffs, it’s a tax hike the company can’t price in. 
What JLR does have going for it in China is the strength of brand, with the Defender particularly still the benchmark design seven years after launch.
“I probably will not exaggerate if I say there's 100 copycats of Defender [in China],” chief commercial officer Leonard Hoornik said. “But if you are in a Defender at a traffic light and there is a lookalike Defender next to you, the magic of what we do is that person looks at you and thinks 'I want to be in that Defender', and you, in a real Defender, you look at that car and say ‘I would never want to be in that’.”

title: US offers $10 million for info on group behind Signal and WhatsApp hacking spree
description: Operation by two Russia-state groups has been ongoing since at least March.
content:
Federal authorities are offering a reward of up to $10 million for information leading to the identification or location of a Russian state cyber group that has compromised thousands of Signal and WhatsApp accounts belonging to investigative reporters and US government employees.
The operation has been active since at least March, when the FBI published an advisory warning of ongoing phishing campaigns targeting high-value targets by attackers associated with Russian intelligence services. Messages masquerading as automated support communications ask that users click a link or provide verification codes or account passcodes. In the event the user complies, they unknowingly link the attacker's device to their account or have their account completely taken over and are locked out.

title: How protein blend and veg collagen peptide is creating a new category in India's nutrition industry
description: New Delhi, June 2026 – India's sports nutrition industry is evolving beyond conventional protein supplementation. Consumers today are increasingly looking for products that align with broader wellness goals rather than focusing solely on muscle growth. As awareness around recovery, active lifestyles, and long-term health continues to grow, demand is rising for formulations that deliver multiple benefits through a single product. This shift in consumer behavior is creating opportunities for innovation, encouraging brands to rethink traditional supplementation. Recognizing this shift, Proathlix developed Protein Blend & Veg Collagen Peptide, a formulation that reflects the growing demand for more comprehensive approaches to nutrition and wellness.The Idea That Inspired Protein Blend & Veg Collagen PeptideFounded in 2017, Proathlix has focused on developing science-backed sports nutrition products for India's growing fitness community. As the market matured, the brand observed that most protein supplements were designed around a single objective—meeting daily protein requirements. However, consumers were becoming increasingly aware of the importance of recovery, consistency, and overall wellness. Recognizing this shift, Proathlix set out to create a formulation that could offer more than conventional protein supplementation. This vision ultimately led to the development of Protein Blend & Veg Collagen Peptide.Identifying a Gap in the MarketIndia's protein supplement market offers consumers a wide variety of choices, ranging from whey concentrates and isolates to plant-based alternatives. Yet most products continue to rely on one or two primary protein sources, with formulations largely focused on meeting basic protein requirements. While these products serve specific nutritional purposes, they often fail to address the broader needs of individuals pursuing active lifestyles.Modern consumers are no longer evaluating supplements based solely on protein content. They are increasingly interested in ingredient quality, digestibility, recovery support, sustained nutrition, and overall wellness benefits. This shift in expectations highlighted an opportunity for a more comprehensive approach to supplementation. Rather than depending on a single protein source, there was growing potential for a formulation that could combine multiple protein sources with complementary ingredients. The gap in the market was not simply about protein intake but about nutritional completeness. This thinking ultimately led to the development of Protein Blend & Veg Collagen Peptide, a formulation that brings together nine protein sources within a single nutritional solution designed to support the evolving needs of today's consumers.The Limitations of Traditional Protein SupplementationConventional protein supplements are typically formulated to deliver amino acids that support muscle repair and growth. While effective for this purpose, many products are designed with a narrow nutritional focus. As fitness culture expands beyond competitive bodybuilding and professional sports, consumers are beginning to expect more from their daily supplementation routines.Athletes, working professionals, and fitness enthusiasts often seek nutritional products that fit into a holistic wellness strategy. Recovery, sustained nutrition, and everyday physical resilience have become increasingly important considerations. This changing landscape has encouraged innovation within the industry and highlighted the need for formulations that go beyond traditional protein delivery.The Science Behind the FormulationThe development of Protein Blend & Veg Collagen Peptide was guided by a simple observation: consumers are increasingly seeking nutritional solutions that offer more than a single functional benefit. As awareness around recovery, consistency, and long-term wellness grows, the industry is witnessing a shift from single-purpose supplements toward more comprehensive formulations.Built around the concept of nutritional diversity, Protein Blend & Veg Collagen Peptide combines nine protein sources with veg collagen peptides to support the evolving needs of modern consumers. By bringing together different forms of protein within a single formulation, the product reflects a broader movement in functional nutrition—one that prioritizes sustained nutritional support, convenience, and a more holistic approach to wellness.The Rise of Multifunctional NutritionConsumer expectations from nutritional products have evolved significantly over the past decade. Today's fitness enthusiasts are increasingly looking for products that fit into broader wellness routines rather than addressing a single objective. Recovery, mobility, sustained energy, and overall physical well-being are becoming equally important considerations alongside muscle growth and performance.This shift is encouraging innovation across the industry and creating demand for formulations that can deliver multiple benefits within a single product. As a result, multifunctional nutrition is emerging as one of the most significant trends shaping the future of sports supplementation.Market Opportunity and the Future of Functional NutritionIndia's health and wellness sector continues to experience strong growth, driven by increasing fitness awareness, preventive health practices, and changing lifestyle priorities. Consumers are becoming more informed about nutrition and increasingly willing to invest in products that align with long-term wellness goals. This trend is being supported by rising health awareness, growing participation in fitness activities, and a stronger focus on preventive wellness across consumer segments.As the industry evolves, products that combine performance nutrition with broader wellness benefits are expected to gain greater attention. Through Protein Blend & Veg Collagen Peptide, Proathlix aims to contribute to this shift with a formulation that reflects changing consumer expectations. The growing interest in comprehensive nutritional solutions suggests that the future of supplementation may be defined not by single-purpose products, but by integrated formulations designed for modern lifestyles.Yuvraj Bali, Founder of Proathlix, explains the vision behind the formulation: "We observed that consumers were no longer evaluating supplements solely on protein content. Their expectations had evolved toward solutions that could support performance, recovery, and overall wellness within a single routine. Protein Blend & Veg Collagen Peptide was developed from that understanding and our belief that the future of nutrition will be shaped by greater nutritional diversity and functionality.”As consumer expectations continue to evolve, the nutrition industry is likely to witness greater innovation around multifunctional formulations. Products that combine performance-focused nutrition with broader wellness support may play an increasingly important role in shaping the future of supplementation in India.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: Jeep to launch Chinese 4x4 flagship and two small SUVs in Europe
description:
Two smaller SUVs will use new Stellantis platform; 4x4 flagship will be a Dongfeng production


American brand rethinks its European strategy as it primes a line-up of five multi-energy models by 2030

Jeep will overhaul its European product line-up with the launch of three new models in the next four years, including two small crossovers developed specifically for the region and a new large SUV co-developed with Chinese firm Dongfeng.
The three new cars, which will all be multi-energy models and have a four-wheel drive option, will sit alongside the existing Avenger compact crossover and mid-sized Compass, with the goal of helping the American brand substantially grow its European sales.
Jeep is part of the Stellantis empire and one of its four designated ‘global’ brands, which means it will take priority in terms of product investment.
But while Jeep’s European strategy has previously focused on models taken from its core American range (joined by the occasional regional addition such as the Avenger), it will now develop a largely bespoke line-up – potentially with the Compass as its only model also sold in the US.
Fabio Catone, Jeep’s European boss, said that the three new arrivals would all build on the brand’s existing design pillars of capability, protection and versatility, and offer 4x4 ability.
He vowed they would represent what “makes a Jeep a Jeep, so the values, the product formula, the off-road heritage and our unique design language – at the end of the day, our identity”.
Small SUVs to focus on key Euro market
Jeep’s two new B-segment SUVs will sit above the existing Avenger, with one being described as "compact" and the other as "large".
Catone said that while the Avenger is positioned to win over buyers of B-segment hatchbacks, the compact B-SUV will be “wider, much more SUV-style”, while the larger B-SUV will be closer in size to the C-segment, which means it will sit just below the existing Compass.

Both will sit on the new STLA One platform that Stellantis is introducing across its brands and both will be manufactured in Europe.
Jeep has traditionally had mixed fortunes developing cars specifically for Europe: the Renegade compact SUV sold well but lacked the authentic off-road capability the brand was known for, while the Avenger is currently its best-seller and a former Car of the Year award winner.
“Jeep is a global brand with values that are shared all over the world,” said Catone. “The product formula is the same all over the world, but we are a global company with strong regional tools, and so we are developing a line-up that is strongly oriented to European customers’ needs.”
Catone hinted that the next-generation STLA One platform is being developed to ensure Jeep can deliver the four-wheel-drive ability that it's known for: “It’s not that we are developing on something [that is] existing that we have to adapt; we are working on something new. It's a shared platform globally, but we have the opportunity to create these cars from scratch, so we can implement all of our requirements as a Jeep.
“We are very confident that we will be able to deliver a true Jeep experience and benefit from the manufacturing and the industrial efficiency of the co-operation within the group.”
Catone said that the new platform is being engineered to ensure that the 4WD of the new Jeeps will be able to offer “best-in-class” off-road ability, with “specific investments” being made to offer the technical ability and approach and departure angles requited.
He hinted that could mean the new Jaapes will feature technology that will be enabled on STLA One such as steer-by-wire, which is being introduced by sister brand Peugeot in its future line-up.
Chinese joint venture to supply new flagship
Jeep’s new large SUV will be produced in a joint venture with Chinese firm Dongfeng, which is already partnering with Stellantis on a pair of new global Peugeot models.
Catone said the new machine, which will be built in China on a Dongfeng platform, will feature a design entirely set by Jeep and will still be engineered for off-road ability.
He described it as a “global Jeep project that will be sold in China and other regions, including Europe” but insisted it's a “vehicle fully designed by Jeep”.
He said it's similar to “an iPhone model, in which the technical features of the design are fully lined with the identity of the brand but we leverage on the industrial footprint”.
The D-SUV will also be multi-energy, with a plug-in hybrid option that's likely to be based on Dongfeng’s range-extender technology.
The Chinese company recently established M-Hero as a similarly positioned off-road brand, which is a likely candidate to provide the basis for Jeep's new European flagship.
M-Hero was launched in 2023 with the 917, a relatively extreme off-roader, and followed it up last year with the M817 (below), a 5.1m-long family SUV with rugged styling and four-wheel drive. 

Available in China with either a PHEV or a REx drivetrain and up to 900bhp combined, the M817 bears a strong resemblance to the D-SUV silhouette shown in Jeep's strategy presentation and would seem best placed for sale in Europe as a rival to the Land Rover Defender and Toyota Land Cruiser.
Jeep briefly offered the large, American-built Grand Cherokee in Europe but ditched that more upmarket model due to slow sales.
Asked if a large Jeep could find success in Europe, Catone said: “The D-SUV marked in Europe for us is a white space, and globally it’s a very strong pillar for Jeep. Our position is to add a strong D-SUV proposition, and we found this a strong opportunity with Dongfeng.”
Jeep currently doesn't sell the fabled Wrangler 4x4 in Europe, due to emissions regulations, and Catone said the firm is still looking for a way to reintroduce it in the future.
No mention was made of the US-built Recon and Wagoneer S electric SUVs, which suggests that an earlier plan to bring them here has been put on ice.

title: Electricity generation lifts India’s Industrial Growth to 5.1 percent in May
description: India’s Index of Industrial Production (IIP) expanded 5.1 percent year-on-year in May 2026, driven by robust momentum in manufacturing, water supply and a surge in electricity generation, according to government data released by the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MOSPI) on Monday.The headline figure marks a pickup from the 4.9 percent growth recorded in April. The May figure is also higher than the 3.4 percent growth recorded in the same month last year.The May reading is the second data release under the new IIP series with the base year of 2022-23, which MOSPI first published on June 1. Alongside the headline numbers, the Ministry announced a significant methodological revision by switching from the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) to the Output Producer Price Index as the deflator, a price index that strips out the effect of inflation to reveal actual production volumes, for 234 of the 463 item groups in the basket. The change supersedes the earlier WPI-based series. “This has led to material changes in growth across segments such as manufacturing and is also likely to lead to revisions in the GDP data,” says Rahul Agrawal, principal economist at ICRA.Also Read: New IIP Series Shows India’s Industrial Production Rising 4.9% in AprilElectricity Surges, Mining SlipsElectricity and gas supply was the fastest-growing major sector, recording 9.9 percent growth in May, driven by elevated temperatures as well as a low base. Within the segment, renewable electricity generation rose 18 percent, continuing the strong momentum seen in April. Electricity from non-renewable sources grew 8.8 percent, higher than the 2.8 percent growth seen in the previous month. Overall, electricity generation grew 11.1 percent, though gas supply contracted 7.1 percent. “This segment pushed up the headline IIP print by as much as 57 basis points between these months,” says Agrawal.Manufacturing output grew 5.5 percent in May, with 16 of the 23 industry groups posting growth. The May figure was lower than the 6.1 percent growth recorded by the sector in April. The standout performers were electrical equipment, which surged 20.8 percent; motor vehicles, trailers and semi-trailers, which rose 14.5 percent; and the manufacture of fabricated metal products, which grew by 15.5 percent. Basic metals registered a more modest 4.6 percent growth.A government note released along with the data shows that within the automobile sector, passenger cars, commercial vehicles and auto components all contributed meaningfully to growth, signalling that domestic demand for vehicles remains firm. In electrical equipment, switchgear and circuit breakers, transformers and UPS systems were among the key drivers, reflecting continued investment in power infrastructure.Within manufacturing, some sectors showed a contraction in output, such as textiles (-1.6 percent), wearing apparel (-8.8 percent), leather and wood products (-4.1 percent each), printing of recorded media (-10.3 percent), the manufacture of petroleum products (-4.7 percent) and the manufacture of chemical products (-1.3 percent).Water supply, sewerage and waste management posted 5.5 percent growth in May, with sewerage and waste management, at 6.9 percent, outpacing water supply, at 4.3 percent.Mining and quarrying was the lone weak spot, contracting 1.6 percent. Output from fuel minerals and non-metallic minerals both contracted by over 6 percent, offsetting a strong 18.3 percent increase in metallic minerals, including rare earth minerals.Investment Goods Outpace ConsumptionOn a use-based breakdown, capital goods emerged as the strongest category, with growth of 12.9 percent, pointing to continued investment activity in the economy. Infrastructure and construction goods rose 5.9 percent, consumer durables climbed 7.2 percent, and intermediate goods grew 5.8 percent. Primary goods and consumer non-durables were the laggards, at 2.6 percent and 3.6 percent respectively.

title: Relocating trust: Why India is no longer an alternative
description: In the boardrooms of global corporations, one phrase has followed India for decades: Cost arbitrage.It was a polite way of saying that India's main advantage was its vast reservoir of English-speaking graduates willing to do what their counterparts in New York, Frankfurt or Tokyo simply could not afford to do at scale. For a long time, that narrative served its purpose. It built an industry, seeded an ecosystem and put India on the map of global commerce.But that story is now being quietly and irrevocably retired.What is replacing it is something far more consequential and durable. The decisive factor in where global corporations choose to plant their intellectual and operational flags is no longer how much they save. It is how much they can trust. The question CEOs are asking has changed. It is no longer, 'Where is it cheapest?' It now is, 'Where is it safest to invest?'Also Read: India’s GCC story going “positively”: Nirmala SitharamanThe Architecture of UncertaintyWe are navigating one of the most significant realignments of the global economic order since 1991, as supply chains that were built purely on efficiency have proven somewhat brittle.While recent global pandemics and escalating geopolitical tensions, including situations in West Asia, which serve as temporary blips, they have underscored a broader need for structural resilience. The initial reactionary strategy of ‘China+1’ may now be hitting a wall.The framework treated emerging markets as interchangeable fallbacks. The narrative has now fundamentally shifted. Capital and intellectual property are no longer seeking a geopolitical hedge. They are seeking a high-trust default, a standard that India now uniquely defines.Manufacturing Has Found a New LogicIndia's manufacturing story was earlier underwritten by two arguments: Demographic scale and policy incentives. Both continue to remain relevant, but they are no longer the main drivers. Today, global manufacturers across electronics, pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, and precision engineering are being drawn to India for something harder to replicate and easier to trust. Global corporations are increasingly drawn by India's stable, rules-based environment, a deepening alignment with Western supply chains, and an institutional framework that they can navigate with confidence.The production-linked incentive schemes have catalysed investment across 14 sectors, attracting global names in mobile manufacturing, speciality chemicals, medical devices and solar modules. But the strategic shift is more significant. India is now part of global conversations about critical supply chains, including semiconductors, aerospace components and defence manufacturing, not as a cost destination, but as a geopolitical partner.Also Read: India's moment is not about austerity. It is about productive powerThe Digital Infrastructure BetThere are very few signals of structural confidence as strong as a data centre investment. And India is witnessing one of the highest investment commitments to digital infrastructure in the Asia-Pacific region. Hyperscalers, global technology firms and institutional capital are committing to India's data centre ecosystem at a pace that shows their long-term conviction.This is not accidental. The country's data localisation framework, growing regulatory alignment with global data governance norms and its large digitally active population make it both a strategic necessity and a commercial imperative. For global corporations across multiple jurisdictions, India's maturing digital infrastructure offers something rare: Scale with trust.Intellectual Capital Is Moving TooPerhaps the most underappreciated dimension of India's rise is the nature of what is actually being relocated. The economy of intellectual capital, including R&D mandates, product development ownership, AI and engineering leadership, is shifting to India in an unprecedented manner.The most legible proof of this is the fast-evolving ecosystem of Global Capability Centres (GCCs). GCCs accounted for around 35 to 40 percent of total office absorption in India in 2025. In 2026 too, they are likely to have a share of over 40 percent. These are not back-office expansions. They are strategic bets: centres co-owning product development, driving R&D mandates and, in some cases, functioning as secondary headquarters for entire business verticals. Sectors that were once peripheral to this conversation, namely semiconductors, aerospace and advanced manufacturing, are now among its fastest-growing contributors.The startup ecosystem in the country further reinforces the thesis from a different perspective. With one of the largest pools of technology startups globally and a rapidly growing pipeline of deep-tech ventures in areas from space to quantum computing, India is not only executing global ideas but actively generating them.A Trust Geography Is Taking ShapeA common thread emerges all these vectors. This time, India's rise as a primary global node is qualitatively different from its earlier emergence as an outsourcing hub. The nature of what is being committed is materially different: intellectual property, data, strategic infrastructure and long-cycle R&D. You do not put these in a low-trust geography.For the better part of the last decade, India has worked on building the institutional infrastructure to meet these demands. Its data protection framework is maturing. Its position as a preferred partner in Western semiconductor supply chains is strengthening. Its democratic credentials, common law tradition and deepening integration into the rules-based international order are evolving from diplomatic talking points into commercially legible factors.In a world where the old rules of globalisation are under stress, markets that offer structural stability may acquire a premium. India is in a strong position to earn that premium.The Decade AheadThe era of viewing global expansion through a comparative lens may well be over. The location of intellectual capital and future corporate growth is not dictated by a distinct, self-sustaining logic.Global boards are no longer seeking a temporary hedge to mitigate risk; they are seeking a foundational anchor for their global operations. India has unequivocally emerged as that answer, and increasingly, the world's most critical enterprises are architecting their futures around this new reality.(The author is the Chairman & CEO—India, South-East Asia, Middle East & Africa, CBRE)

title: How Abdulaziz Osman is building the infrastructure layer behind the digital economy
description: The next decade will not be defined solely by software, artificial intelligence, or digital assets.It will be defined by the infrastructure powering them.Behind every artificial intelligence model, every cloud application, every digital payment, and every blockchain transaction lies a vast ecosystem of energy systems, data centers, computing hardware, networking infrastructure, and operational expertise.As the global economy becomes increasingly digital, the organizations building this infrastructure are becoming some of the most strategically important businesses in the world.Among the companies operating at the intersection of this transformation is BitHash, a UAE-based digital infrastructure company founded by entrepreneur Abdulaziz Osman.Over the past several years, BitHash has evolved from a digital asset infrastructure operator into a broader platform focused on computing infrastructure, renewable energy integration, hosting solutions, and next-generation digital infrastructure ecosystems.Today, the company reports:50 MW deployed or under developmentMore than 10,000 ASIC miners deployedOperations across five global locations20 MW renewable energy capacity30 MW major infrastructure milestones achievedWhile these numbers reflect significant growth, Abdulaziz Osman believes they represent only the beginning of a much larger opportunity."We are entering a new infrastructure cycle," he says."Artificial intelligence, data centers, digital assets, and energy are converging into a single ecosystem. The organizations that build and operate this infrastructure will help define the future global economy."Chapter One: The Entrepreneurial JourneyEvery entrepreneur's story begins long before their company is founded.For Abdulaziz Osman, the journey began with a simple observation:The world's greatest opportunities are often hidden beneath the surface.Like many ambitious professionals, Osman began his career in the corporate world.The environment offered stability, structure, and predictability.Yet as global technological transformation accelerated, he found himself increasingly drawn toward entrepreneurship.What fascinated him was not simply technology itself.It was the infrastructure behind it.Throughout history, every major economic transformation has depended upon infrastructure.Railroads powered industrialization.Electricity transformed manufacturing.Telecommunications connected nations.The internet reshaped commerce.Cloud computing revolutionized enterprise technology.Infrastructure always came first.The products and services that captured public attention were built upon foundations created years earlier.This realization would ultimately become the foundation upon which BitHash was built."The biggest opportunities often exist where few people are looking," Osman explains."Everyone sees the finished product. Few people focus on the infrastructure making it possible."Leaving corporate life was not easy.Entrepreneurship required risk.It required uncertainty.It required resilience.Like most founders, Osman encountered setbacks, unexpected challenges, and moments when progress seemed slow.Yet those experiences reinforced a philosophy that continues guiding him today:Consistency beats intensity.Discipline beats motivation.Execution beats theory."Success rarely comes from one breakthrough moment," he says."It comes from showing up every day, solving problems, and continuing forward when outcomes remain uncertain."Chapter Two: Seeing Beyond BitcoinWhen many people hear the phrase Bitcoin mining, they immediately think of cryptocurrency.They think about price charts.Market cycles.Volatility.Speculation.Abdulaziz Osman sees something entirely different.He sees infrastructure.Behind every Bitcoin transaction exists a sophisticated ecosystem of energy systems, networking architecture, cooling technologies, data centers, logistics, and operational management.The deeper Osman studied the industry, the more convinced he became that the true long-term opportunity was not speculation.It was infrastructure.This distinction fundamentally shaped BitHash's strategy.While others focused on market cycles, BitHash focused on building systems.While others followed short-term trends, BitHash concentrated on long-term infrastructure development."Bitcoin mining is no longer simply a cryptocurrency business," Osman says."It is an infrastructure business."This philosophy enabled BitHash to position itself for opportunities extending far beyond digital assets.Infrastructure remains valuable regardless of market cycles.Computing power remains necessary.Energy remains necessary.Data centers remain necessary.The future belongs to those who build and manage these systems efficiently.Chapter Three: The Rise of the Infrastructure EconomyThroughout history, infrastructure has driven economic growth.Today, another infrastructure revolution is underway.Artificial intelligence.Data centers.Cloud computing.Digital assets.Renewable energy.High-performance computing.These sectors increasingly depend upon one another.Together they form what Osman describes as the Infrastructure Economy.Artificial intelligence requires data centers.Data centers require energy.Energy requires infrastructure.Digital assets require computing power.Computing power requires cooling systems and networking architecture.The industries are no longer operating independently.They are converging.Organizations capable of managing these interconnected systems may become some of the most influential companies of the coming decade.This convergence forms the foundation of BitHash's long-term vision.The company does not see itself solely as a mining operator.It sees itself as a digital infrastructure company.One capable of supporting multiple computing ecosystems as demand continues expanding globally.Chapter Four: Artificial Intelligence and the Computing RevolutionFew technologies have generated as much excitement as artificial intelligence.From large language models and autonomous systems to enterprise automation and predictive analytics, AI is transforming nearly every industry.Yet AI is fundamentally an infrastructure story.Training advanced AI models requires enormous computing resources.Deploying those models requires:Data centersHigh-performance computing hardwareNetworking infrastructureCooling technologiesReliable powerEvery advancement in AI increases demand for infrastructure.Industry forecasts suggest AI-related infrastructure investment could exceed hundreds of billions of dollars globally over the coming decade.This transformation presents significant opportunities for infrastructure operators.The expertise required to manage large-scale computing environments increasingly applies across multiple sectors.Whether supporting AI, blockchain, cloud computing, or enterprise applications, the underlying requirements remain remarkably similar.Energy.Computing power.Cooling.Connectivity.Operational excellence.These capabilities form the foundation of BitHash's long-term strategy."We believe AI will become one of the largest infrastructure opportunities of our generation," Osman says."The demand for computing power is only beginning."Chapter Five: Data Centers Become Strategic AssetsIn many ways, data centers have become the factories of the digital age.Every online transaction.Every cloud application.Every AI query.Every enterprise platform.Every digital asset transaction.Depends upon data center infrastructure.Without data centers, the modern digital economy simply cannot function.As digital transformation accelerates globally, institutional investors increasingly view data centers as strategic assets.The sector continues attracting significant investment from:Private equity firmsInfrastructure fundsTechnology companiesSovereign wealth fundsFamily officesThe rationale is straightforward.Computing demand continues growing.Computing demand requires infrastructure.BitHash believes this trend represents one of the defining investment themes of the next decade.The company continues evaluating opportunities within digital infrastructure ecosystems capable of supporting future demand across multiple industries.Chapter Six: Renewable Energy StrategyEnergy is becoming one of the most important competitive advantages in the digital economy.Without energy, computing cannot operate.Without affordable energy, computing cannot scale.Without sustainable energy, long-term growth becomes increasingly difficult.Recognizing this reality, BitHash has incorporated renewable energy into its infrastructure roadmap.The company currently reports 20 MW of renewable energy capacity.For Osman, renewable energy is not simply an environmental initiative.It is an infrastructure strategy.Organizations capable of integrating energy production with computing infrastructure may enjoy significant long-term advantages."Energy will be one of the defining strategic assets of the next decade," Osman says."The companies that successfully integrate infrastructure and energy strategy will be positioned to grow."Chapter Seven: Building at ScaleInfrastructure development requires execution.Vision alone is not enough.Success requires planning.Capital allocation.Operational excellence.Risk management.Over the years, BitHash has focused on building infrastructure capable of supporting long-term growth.Today, the company reports:Key Milestones50 MW deployed or under developmentMore than 10,000 ASIC miners deployedOperations spanning five global locations20 MW renewable energy capacity30 MW major infrastructure milestones achievedThese achievements represent years of deployment, operational management, and infrastructure development.For Osman, however, they are milestones rather than destinations."We are still early in our journey," he says."The infrastructure opportunity ahead is significantly larger than what has already been achieved."Chapter Eight: Global ExpansionThe future of infrastructure is global.Technology moves globally.Capital moves globally.Energy markets operate globally.Computing demand is global.As a result, BitHash has pursued an international growth strategy.Today, the company operates across five global locations.The UAE remains central to this strategy.Its strategic location, business-friendly environment, and commitment to innovation make it an ideal foundation for international expansion.From this base, BitHash continues evaluating opportunities across emerging and established markets.The objective is not simply geographic expansion.It is strategic positioning.Building infrastructure where long-term demand is expected to grow.Chapter Nine: Leadership and CultureEvery organization reflects its leadership.At BitHash, culture is shaped by several core principles:Resilience.Accountability.Execution.Long-term thinking.Entrepreneurship requires navigating uncertainty.Markets evolve.Technology changes.Challenges emerge.Organizations capable of adapting while maintaining focus often achieve sustainable success.According to Osman, culture ultimately determines execution.And execution determines outcomes."Great businesses are not built overnight," he says."They are built through thousands of small decisions made consistently over time."Chapter Ten: Looking Toward the FutureThe world is entering a new infrastructure era.Artificial intelligence continues expanding.Data center demand continues growing.Renewable energy adoption continues accelerating.Digital assets continue maturing.Each trend reinforces the importance of infrastructure.For BitHash, the future lies at the intersection of these industries.The company continues investing in infrastructure development, energy integration, computing ecosystems, and international expansion.Its objective is not merely to participate in technological transformation.Its objective is to help build the systems enabling it."The next generation of digital infrastructure will be built where energy, computing power, and capital intersect," Osman says."The organizations that understand this convergence today will help define the global economy tomorrow."About Abdulaziz OsmanAbdulaziz Osman is the Founder and Chief Executive Officer of BitHash. He is recognized for his work in digital infrastructure, Bitcoin mining, renewable energy integration, data center development, and next-generation computing ecosystems.About BitHashBitHash is a UAE-based digital infrastructure company focused on Bitcoin mining, hosting solutions, renewable energy integration, data center development, and digital infrastructure services.Company Milestones50 MW deployed or under development10,000+ ASIC miners deployed5 global operating locations20 MW renewable energy capacity30 MW infrastructure milestones achievedLinkedin :-   www.linkedin.com/in/abdulaziz-osman-crypto-mining-expertWebsite :-  www.bithash.aeEmail:-  Info@bithash.aeThis is sponsored content and has not been written by Forbes India journalists. Forbes India has not independently verified the information contained herein and makes no representation regarding its accuracy, completeness, or reliability. Readers should make their independent inquiries and take informed decisions.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: ഗുണ്ടാ നേതാക്കളും ക്രിമിനലുകളും പങ്കെടുത്തു; കെ. സുധാകരൻ വിളിച്ചുചേർത്ത യോ​ഗം അറിഞ്ഞിട്ടില്ലെന്ന് ഡിസിസി ​
description: മട്ടാഞ്ചേരി ഹാരിസ്, തോക്ക് കേസ് പ്രതി റിയാസ് അടക്കം യോഗത്തിൽ പങ്കെടുത്തു. യോഗത്തിന്‍റെ ദൃശ്യങ്ങൾ സമൂഹ മാധ്യമങ്ങളിൽ പ്രചരിച്ചിരുന്നു.
content: <p><strong>കൊച്ചി:</strong> ഓപ്പറേഷൻ തൂഫാന് ഐക്യദാർഢ്യം പ്രഖ്യാപിച്ച് കെ സുധാകരകൻ എംപി കൊച്ചിയിൽ വിളിച്ച് ചേർത്ത യോഗം തങ്ങളുടെ അറിവോടെ അല്ലെന്ന് എറണാകുളം ഡിസിസി. ക്രിമിനൽ കേസ് പ്രതികളെയും ഗുണ്ടാ നേതാക്കളേയും പങ്കെടുപ്പിച്ച് കൊണ്ടായിരുന്നു യോഗം. യോഗത്തിന് മുമ്പോ ശേഷമോ കൂടിയാലോചന നടത്തിയിട്ടില്ലെന്ന് ഡിസിസി വൃത്തങ്ങൾ അറിയിച്ചു. മട്ടാഞ്ചേരി ഹാരിസ്, തോക്ക് കേസ് പ്രതി റിയാസ് അടക്കം യോഗത്തിൽ പങ്കെടുത്തു. യോഗത്തിന്‍റെ ദൃശ്യങ്ങൾ സമൂഹ മാധ്യമങ്ങളിൽ പ്രചരിച്ചിരുന്നു. നടന്നത് അനൗദ്യോഗിക യോഗം മാത്രമെന്നാണ് സുധാകരനോട് അടുത്ത വൃത്തങ്ങൾ പറയുന്നത്.&nbsp;</p><p>മട്ടാഞ്ചേരി ഹാരിസ്, തോക്ക് കേസ് പ്രതി റിയാസ്, പുലി നസീർ, ബ്യുട്ടീപാർലർ വെടിവെപ്പ് കേസിലെ പ്രതി ബിലാൽ തുടങ്ങി ക്രമിനൽ കേസ് പ്രതികളും മുൻ ഗുണ്ടാ നേതാക്കളും യോഗത്തിൽ പങ്കെടുത്തു. ആഭ്യന്തരവകുപ്പും എക്സൈസ് വകുപ്പും നടപ്പിലാക്കുന്ന ഓപ്പറേഷൻ തൂഫാൻ, ഓപ്പറേഷൻ തണ്ടർ പരിപാടികൾക്ക് പിന്തുണ എന്ന പേരിലാണ് കൊച്ചിയിൽ കൂട്ടായ്മ നടന്നത്. സംസ്ഥാന സർക്കാർ പരിശ്രമങ്ങളുടെ ഭാഗമാകാൻ യോഗത്തിൽ കെ സുധാകരൻ ആവശ്യപ്പെട്ടു. യോഗത്തിന്‍റെ ദൃശ്യങ്ങൾ സമൂഹ മാധ്യമങ്ങളിൽ പ്രചരിച്ചതിന് പിന്നാലെയാണ് കോൺഗ്രസ് നേതാക്കൾക്കിടയിൽ നിന്ന് തന്നെ വിമർശനം ഉയരുന്നത്.&nbsp;</p><p>കോൺഗ്രസിലെ മുതിർന്ന നേതാവ് ക്രമിനൽ കേസ് പശ്ചാത്തലമുള്ളവർക്കൊപ്പം എത്തിയത് എന്തിനെന്നാണ് ചോദ്യം.യോഗം ചേർന്നത് പാർട്ടിയുടെ അറിവോടെ അല്ലെന്നാണ് എറണാകുളം ഡിസിസിയുടെ വിശദീകരണം. ഇത്തരം ഒരു യോഗം കൊച്ചിയിൽ വിളിച്ച് ചേർക്കുന്നതിന് മുൻപോ ശേഷമോ പാർട്ടിയുമായി ഒരു കൂടിയാലോചനയും സുധാകരൻ നടത്തിയിരുന്നില്ലെന്നും ഡിസിസി നേതാക്കൾ വ്യക്തമാക്കി. ഔദ്യോഗികമായി വിളിച്ച് ചേർത്ത യോഗം ആയിരുന്നില്ലെന്നും തികച്ചും അനൗദ്യോഗികം ആയിരുന്നെന്നുമാണ് സുധാകരനോട് അടുത്ത വൃത്തങ്ങളുടെ പ്രതികരണം.</p>

title: Metallica donate £20,000 to city foodbank with 'empty shelves' after sell-out gig
description: Metallica's donation is enough for 9000 meals, as they performed their record-breaking M72 World Tour.

title: Brain activity under anesthesia challenges what we know about consciousness
description: The unconscious brain appears to be far more capable than scientists once believed. Researchers found that patients under general anesthesia could still process language at a sophisticated level, distinguishing nouns, verbs, and adjectives while listening to stories. Even more remarkably, neural activity showed signs of predicting upcoming words before they were heard. The results challenge traditional ideas about consciousness and hint at new possibilities for brain-computer interfaces.

title: These tiny soil microbes could rescue crops from salty farmland
description: Researchers have discovered that beneficial soil bacteria give plants an unexpected survival advantage in salty soils. Instead of helping plants keep salt out, the microbes stimulate the production of lignin, a natural compound that strengthens roots and makes plants more resilient. Greenhouse and field tests showed healthier plants and higher yields in salty conditions. The findings could lead to bio-based treatments that help farmers grow crops on land once considered too salty for agriculture.

title: Trump Cut a Billion-Dollar Mining Deal. His Sons Stand to Profit.
description: An agreement between the U.S. and Kazakhstan has given a group of American investors with ties to the president and the commerce secretary access to one of the world’s largest untapped reserves of tungsten.

title: Hawaii is turning ocean plastic and fishing nets into roads
description: Hawaii researchers are giving old fishing nets and recycled plastic a second life by mixing them into asphalt roads. Early tests found these roads didn't release more plastic particles than standard pavement, with tire wear overwhelming any plastic signal from the recycled material. If future studies confirm the roads are durable, the technology could help tackle both marine pollution and overflowing landfills.

title: This 'postcard-pretty' seaside town has been named the UK's best babymoon destination
description: From coastal retreats to countryside escapes, a new ranking has revealed the five best UK babymoon destinations

title: Heatwaves bring these 5 household pests into your home – here's how to keep them out
description: Heatwaves can lead to a spike in household pests. Here's how to keep the most common culprits at bay

title: Scientists discover what triggers belly fat as we age
description: Aging may trigger the appearance of specialized stem cells that supercharge the body's ability to create new belly fat. The discovery reveals a potential biological driver of middle-age weight gain and a promising target for future anti-obesity treatments.

title: Astronomers found two rare super puff planets lighter than cotton candy
description: Two newly confirmed "super-puff" planets are so diffuse that they are less dense than cotton candy, despite being about the size of Jupiter. Their rare orbital relationship and enormous, lightweight atmospheres could provide valuable clues about how some of the strangest planets in the galaxy come to exist.

title: Ancient human DNA found on cave art for the first time
description: DNA from ancient humans has been found on a prehistoric cave painting and on cave walls, demonstrating the potential to one day identify individual artists and resolve the debate over Neanderthals' artistic abilities

title: ‘Everyone is talking about Cape Verde’: World Cup run delights diaspora community in UK
description: Cape Verdeans in Britain feeling ‘incredibly proud’ after team’s hard-fought draws against Spain and UruguayWorld Cup live – latest updatesFor as long as she can remember, 13-year-old Lauryn struggled to find a map that included Cape Verde. Now, to her great delight, the tiny African island nation is finally centre stage.“Seeing our country shown across the world at the World Cup makes me feel incredibly proud,” Lauryn says. “After the first match, everyone was talking about Cape Verde. People saw the talent and the skill of our players.” Continue reading...

title: Whereabouts of nearly 300 people with Ebola unknown in DR Congo
description: Fears over ‘huge community transmission’ as modelling predicts thousands of deaths in DRC by SeptemberThe whereabouts of almost 300 people who have tested positive for Ebola in the Democratic Republic of the Congo is unknown, according to Africa’s top public health official.The humanitarian crisis amid the conflict in the affected areas means more than 1 million people are living in camps to which health workers have no access, Dr Jean Kaseya, director general of the Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), said on Thursday. Continue reading...

title: Outrage as woman jailed for three years after criticising Somali government online
description: Sentencing of 27-year-old Sadia Moalim Ali condemned by former president and prime ministers as well as rights groupsA rickshaw driver in Somalia has been sentenced to three years in prison for comments she made on social media, in a case that has caught the public’s attention and provoked outrage in the country.Sadia Moalim Ali, a 27-year-old nursing graduate, was originally charged with insulting government institutions and incitement to commit a crime, but convicted only of the former. Her sentence, immediately condemned as “fundamentally unjust”, was handed down on 25 June. Continue reading...

title: In deep‑red Idaho, even Republicans break with Trump on farm labor
description: A Boise State poll of 1,000 Idaho adults finds 85% support a legal working status pathway for long-term dairy workers — with Republican support at 79%
content:
Under the second Trump administration, the United States has seen mass deportations and a sharp escalation in immigration enforcement. The Department of Homeland Security says the crackdown pushed nearly 3 million people out of the country in Trump’s first year back in office.



For the first time since the 1960s, the number of immigrants living in the U.S. is declining; because most farmworkers are foreign born, those losses are already beginning to strain American farms.



We are social scientists who study immigrant communities in Idaho and the challenges farmworkers face. We also run an annual survey exploring public opinion on a range of policy issues, including immigration and economic conditions. Amid the government’s heated rhetoric, our data shows public opinion on immigration in one of the country’s reddest agricultural states is diverging from national politics and may even be at odds with federal policy.



Immigrant labor in agriculture



According to the Center for Migration Studies, 86% of farmworkers in the U.S. are foreign born, and 45% are undocumented. In 2025 the Trump administration suggested it would not target farms. Still, farmworkers across the country are scared to go to work.



Between March and July 2025, the agricultural workforce declined by 7%, with farms reporting labor shortages in states that voted for Trump, including Pennsylvania, and states that didn’t, including California. Meanwhile, immigration crackdowns are threatening this country’s food security.



National polling on the administration’s immigration policies tends to follow party lines, drawing Democrats’ disapproval and Republicans’ support, but recent polling suggests some softening among the latter. Pew Research Center reported that half of all respondents disapprove of the administration’s approach to immigration, and more than half say Trump is “doing too much” when it comes to deportation. Among Republicans the share is smaller – 20% – but rising.



Research suggests people think about immigration differently at the local level. To explore how public opinion on immigration varies in farm-dependent states, we looked at one of the most agriculture-reliant and Republican states in the country just as its farms face policy-driven labor shortfalls.



A worker carries a crate of freshly picked corn through the field. Immigrant workers make up a large share of the U.S. agricultural workforce. Hill Street Studios/DigitalVision via Getty Images



A closer look



Agribusiness is important for Idaho’s economy, accounting for 20% of its annual GDP, according to the Idaho State Department of Agriculture. Dairy is a cornerstone: The state is the fourth-largest milk producer in the U.S., according to the Idaho Dairymen’s Association. Ninety percent of Idaho dairy workers are foreign born.



Idaho is also a deeply red state: About 60% of voters are registered Republicans, and 67% voted for Trump in 2024. These numbers make Idaho a clear test of how national rhetoric and intensifying immigration enforcement track with public sentiment in places that have the most to lose.



Our 11th annual Idaho Public Policy Survey polled 1,000 adults in the fall of 2025. Because immigrants have lower crime rates than native-born Americans, we designed a question around long-term residents without a criminal record – specifically, dairy workers and their families who have lived in Idaho more than 10 years.



The results showed broad support for a pathway to legal working status for them: 85% of respondents were in favor, with 56% strongly supportive and 29% somewhat. Just 9% opposed. We expected immigration positions to tie directly to political affiliation, but results cut across party lines, with 79% of Republicans, 88% of independents and 95% of Democrats in favor.









We also asked participants whether increased presence from U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, or ICE, would help or harm Idaho’s agricultural economy. More than half – 53% – said it would harm the economy, 19% said it would help, 18% said there would be no impact, and 10% were unsure.



The pattern was sharpest where it would matter most: Residents of agriculture-dependent regions were more likely to say ICE presence would hurt the economy. Republicans were more likely than others to say it would help; even so, only 35% of Republicans responded that way, compared with 11% of independents and less than 3% of Democrats.









In agriculture-dependent Idaho, many residents say they recognize the role immigrants play in sustaining local rural economies, and research shows that recognition can shift attitudes about immigration, potentially increasing support for more inclusive policies.



According to our research, public opinion in Idaho is more nuanced than the right-left divide suggests. We believe findings here are likely to hold in other places similarly dependent on agriculture, as farmers from Wisconsin to Delaware speak up about their reliance on immigrant labor.



Lisa Meierotto, Associate Professor of Global Studies and Environmental Studies, Boise State University; Matthew May, Research Scholar, Boise State University, and Rebecca Som Castellano, Director of Human-Environment Systems and Professor of Sociology, Boise State University



This article is republished from The Conversation under a Creative Commons license. Read the original article.


This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

title: Earth may have been seeding Venus with life for billions of years
description: A new study suggests Earth may have been sending tiny hitchhikers to Venus for billions of years. Researchers found that asteroid impacts could launch microbes into space, where some might survive the journey and end up suspended in Venus' clouds. If future missions detect life there, there's a surprising chance it didn't originate on Venus at all—it may have come from Earth.

title: Medical Journal Retracts Study Claiming Cancer Therapy Is More Effective When Given in the Morning
description: In a notice flagging a series of problems with a clinical trial, the journal Nature Medicine said its editors “no longer have confidence in the integrity of the results.”
content: The journal Nature Medicine retracted a study that found a drastic benefit when cancer therapy was administered in the morning.

title: Einstein Probe may have caught a black hole tearing apart a white dwarf for the first time
description: Astronomers may have witnessed one of the rarest and most dramatic cosmic events ever seen: a long-sought intermediate-mass black hole ripping apart a dense white dwarf star and devouring it. The Einstein Probe space telescope caught the explosion in its earliest moments, revealing an unusual sequence of intense X-ray flashes unlike anything seen in a typical gamma-ray burst.

title: Trump Issues Order to Reduce Pesticides in Food as Kennedy Allies Fume
description: The order, which calls for studying the health risks of pesticides in the food supply, does not involve new federal funding, and does not call for regulations or legislation.
content: President Trump held a Rose Garden dinner with American farmers on Thursday, the same day he issued an executive order aimed at reducing pesticides in the food supply.

title: Congo Ebola Crisis: Contact Tracing Is Dangerously Behind, Officials Warn
description: Most of the people testing positive for Ebola in the Democratic Republic of Congo are not on health workers’ radar, suggesting that contact tracing is lagging dangerously behind.
content: A community health worker taking the temperature of a man whose son is being treated for the virus near the city of Bunia in Democratic Republic of Congo.

title: Inside the C.D.C.’s Mad Scramble to Meet Kennedy’s Demands
description: A cache of internal emails offers a look at the pressure the nation’s public health officials faced from the new health secretary in the early months of the Trump administration.
content: Robert F. Kennedy Jr., seated, at a Senate confirmation hearing last year.

title: Supreme Court Rejects Lawsuit Against Bayer Alleging Roundup Weedkiller Caused Cancer
description: The court’s decision is likely to determine the future of thousands of lawsuits against Bayer, which manufactures the weedkiller, over similar claims.
content: Roundup, which was created in the 1970s, is one of the most popular weedkillers in the world.

title: The Jaguar XJ220 Is Back As A Gorgeous Concept Designed By Ian Callum
description: Callum Designs previews what it says is a modern take on the iconic XJ220. If we’re lucky, it will go into production.

title: The race to understand how and when Thwaites glacier will collapse
description: The loss of Antarctica’s doomsday glacier would transform our planet. Now scientists are revealing the secrets of this remotest of places, and asking the question: is its demise inevitable?

title: Where, when and how to watch the 2026 solar eclipse
description: This August a total solar eclipse is set to be visible across parts of Europe, while a partial eclipse will sweep across about a quarter of the planet – here’s how to catch it

title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
description: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

title: After 70 years of excavation, ancient Sardis becomes a UNESCO World Heritage site
description: After nearly seven decades of excavation, the legendary ancient city of Sardis has become a UNESCO World Heritage Site, celebrating years of discoveries that continue to reshape its history. Archaeologists say the biggest breakthroughs don't happen in a single season—they emerge as decades of evidence slowly come together.

title: NASA’s Lucy finds a wobbling peanut-shaped asteroid with signs of ancient water
description: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft discovered that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped relic born from a violent collision and slowly reshaped by the subtle force of sunlight. It also carries traces of ancient water, making it an important clue to the solar system’s mysterious past.

title: Osteopenia is silently weakening bones in millions of people
description: Osteopenia is a common but often overlooked condition that causes bones to become less dense and more fragile. Because it develops silently, many people only discover they have it after a fracture or bone scan. Aging, menopause, poor diet, and inactivity can all contribute to bone loss. Fortunately, exercise, adequate calcium and vitamin D, and other healthy habits can slow or even partially reverse the decline.

title: France Identifies Its First Case of Ebola
description: A doctor who had traveled to the Democratic Republic of Congo was infected, the French health ministry said. The authorities said the risk to the wider population was low.
content: Health workers last month in the Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of an Ebola outbreak that has killed more than 200 people.

title: Shortage of Chemotherapy Drugs Brings Rationing Fears for Cancer Patients
description: Doctors are contending with low supplies and unfilled orders of generic chemotherapy infusions that are central to the treatment of a long list of cancers.
content: Chemotherapy drugs, carboplatin and cisplatin, in the pharmaceutical storage room at the Center for Cancer and Blood Disorders in Fort Worth. There are shortages of both drugs.

title: Neuroscience can't tell us the way to govern people's brains
description: From the age of legal adulthood to the concept of "profound autism", policy-makers are turning to neuroscience to help shape laws and policies, but the science simply isn't ready

title: The Ebola Outbreak’s Central Mystery: Where Did This Virus Come From?
description: Scientists believe that the Bundibugyo virus persists in an animal species, occasionally spilling over into humans. But they have yet to identify the species.

title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
description: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
description: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

title: Justice Dept. Unveils Medicare Fraud Charges as Part of Larger Health Care Prosecutions
description: The department is taking aim at pricey “skin substitutes,” an overused wound care treatment that cost the government nearly $15 billion in 2025.
content: Mehmet Oz, center, the head of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, with Todd Blanche, right, the acting attorney general, and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. during a briefing at the Justice Department on Tuesday.

title: Ebola Symptoms in Current Outbreak May Be Milder Than in Previous Ones
description: That is good news for patients, but officials fear it will make controlling the spread of the disease harder.
content: A health worker checking people’s temperatures at an entry point to the city of Goma in the Democratic Republic of Congo in May.

title: Pakistan to Cut 18% Period Tax on Menstrual Products, But Poorest Families Still Can’t Afford Them
description: In Pakistan, only 12 percent of women and girls use safe, commercially-made menstrual products. Activists hailed a government plan to cut a “period tax.”
content: In Pakistan, many women and girls are unable to afford commercially made pads and tampons. UNICEF estimates that taxes account for about 40 percent of the price of the products.

title: Doctors Thought It Was Asthma. A.I. Flagged a Serious Heart Problem.
description: Artificial intelligence programs can spot patterns in electrocardiograms that humans miss. Now, one program is going to be widely available — for free — to doctors.
content: A clinical trial using an A.I. program found evidence of possible severe heart damage in the electrocardiogram of a 45-year-old patient.

title: How menopause radically changes the brain – and what happens after
description: The brain undergoes a full renovation during menopause. Although these changes are profound, we’re learning that the long-term impact needn’t be all bad

description: 7 Most venomous snakes in the world that can kill within hours
